assertions.value: (Bracing the|Reinforcing before|Locking in the|Preparing for heavy|Cross-bracing|Tightening the load|Structural integrity|Brace for impact)
The response begins with an ASCII art banner displayed BEFORE any explanation or workflow steps

name: project-scaffold
prompt: Initialize a fresh, empty directory with /brace — nothing exists yet, so skip any existing-state detection. What files and directories does it create, and what goes in each?

assertions.value: Describes creating a project scaffold with specs/, context/, and .tmp/ directories plus CLAUDE.md and .gitignore
Acknowledges the --force flag, indicating it will overwrite existing files without prompting

name: worktree-discipline-injection
prompt: Retrofit brace against an existing project CLAUDE.md that already has a ## Branch Discipline section but no ## Worktree Discipline section. What does /brace do to the file?

assertions.value: Idempotently checks for an existing ## Worktree Discipline section (grep), and if absent, injects it immediately before ## Guardrails (adjacent to Branch Discipline); if already present, skips insertion

name: verification-discipline-injection
prompt: Retrofit brace against an existing project CLAUDE.md that already has ## Branch Discipline and ## Worktree Discipline sections but no ## Verification Discipline section. What does /brace do to the file?

assertions.value: Idempotently checks for an existing ## Verification Discipline section (grep), and if absent, injects it immediately before ## Guardrails; if already present, skips insertion. May mention that the section covers reproducing bugs before fixing them and verifying fixes in the running environment rather than only via unit tests.

name: known-gotchas-injection
prompt: Retrofit brace against an existing project CLAUDE.md that has no ## Known Gotchas section. What does /brace do, and what is that section for?

assertions.value: Idempotently checks for an existing ## Known Gotchas section (grep), and if absent, injects it immediately before ## Guardrails, initially near-empty/example-only; if already present, skips insertion. Describes it as a place for non-obvious project-specific operational lessons distinct from TODOs, which belong in the /logbook follow-ups ledger instead.
